---
title: "MTH 4330 project draft"
subtitle: "Test Code for Group Project"
author: "Raymond Cheung"
date: "2026-03-31"
output: pdf_document
header-includes:
# Add any LaTeX packages you want here -----------------------
- \usepackage{amsmath}
- \usepackage{amssymb}
- \usepackage{graphicx}
---



<!-- Keep this block  -->
```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = FALSE)

# Add any R packages you want here -----------------------
library(rstudioapi)

library(dplyr) #added

library(xtable) #added to export latex tables

# Do not touch below this line ---------------------------
setwd(dirname(rstudioapi::getActiveDocumentContext()$path))
def.chunk.hook  <- knitr::knit_hooks$get("chunk")
knitr::knit_hooks$set(chunk = function(x, options) {
  x <- def.chunk.hook(x, options)
  paste0("\n \\", "footnotesize","\n\n", x, "\n\n \\normalsize")
})
```

```{r install_libraries, echo = TRUE}
#install the library dplyr
#source: https://stackoverflow.com/questions/54621706/error-in-librarydplyr-there-is-no-package-called-dplyr

#comment this out if you already installed it
#install.packages("dplyr")

# something similar for installing xtable
#install.packages("xtable")

```

```{r linear_regression_analysis, echo = TRUE}

#makes the file path easier to change

#data_file_path <- "per_annum_Citywide_Payroll_Data_(Fiscal_Year)_20260227.csv"
#data_file_path <- "per_day_Citywide_Payroll_Data_(Fiscal_Year)_20260227.csv"
#data_file_path <- "per_hour_Citywide_Payroll_Data_(Fiscal_Year)_20260227.csv"
data_file_path <- "prorated_annual_Citywide_Payroll_Data_(Fiscal_Year)_20260227.csv"

#import the data from the csv file
csv_data <- read.csv(data_file_path, header = TRUE, sep = ",")

#clean the data set using the method found at this website
#https://www.statology.org/data-cleaning-in-r/


csv_data <- csv_data %>% na.omit() # removes rows with missing values

# Full disclosure: Most code below this line is CoPilot-assisted.
# The syntax is new to me, so I didn't know what words to search for
# I did my best to made sure that it looked understandable to me and looked up keywords as needed

csv_data <- csv_data %>%
  mutate(
    Regular.Gross.Paid = as.numeric(gsub("[^0-9.]", "", Regular.Gross.Paid)),
    Total.OT.Paid = as.numeric(gsub("[^0-9.]", "", Total.OT.Paid)),
    Regular.Hours      = as.numeric(gsub("[^0-9.]", "", Regular.Hours)),
    Base.Salary        = as.numeric(gsub("[^0-9.]", "", Base.Salary)),
    OT.Hours           = as.numeric(gsub("[^0-9.]", "", OT.Hours)),
    Fiscal.Year        = as.numeric(gsub("[^0-9.]", "", Fiscal.Year))
  )

csv_data <- csv_data %>% 
  filter(
    !is.na(Regular.Gross.Paid),
    !is.na(Total.OT.Paid),
    !is.na(Regular.Hours),
    !is.na(Base.Salary),
    !is.na(OT.Hours),
    !is.na(Fiscal.Year),
    is.finite(Total.OT.Paid),
    is.finite(Regular.Gross.Paid),
    is.finite(Regular.Hours),
    is.finite(Base.Salary),
    is.finite(OT.Hours),
    is.finite(Fiscal.Year)
  )

# Takes fiscal year and centers it around the minimum.
# Also stores the value of the minimum year for later.
# The minimum year will be encoded as 0 in the regression model.
fiscal_year_min <- min(csv_data$Fiscal.Year)
print(fiscal_year_min)
csv_data$Fiscal.Year.Centered <- csv_data$Fiscal.Year - fiscal_year_min

# Creates the Total.Paid variable
csv_data$Total.Paid <- csv_data$Regular.Gross.Paid + csv_data$Total.OT.Paid

#show the first 5 entries
head(csv_data, 10)

#fits a model to the processed dataset
model <- lm(Total.Paid ~ 
              Fiscal.Year.Centered +
              Regular.Hours + 
              Base.Salary + 
              OT.Hours, data = csv_data)

mse_value <- mean((csv_data$Regular.Gross.Paid - predict(model))^2)

#summarize model
summary(model)

# predicted values
csv_data$predicted <- predict(model)

# actual department means
actual_means <- csv_data %>%
  group_by(Agency.Name) %>%
  summarise(actual_mean = mean(Total.Paid, na.rm = TRUE))

# predicted department means
predicted_means <- csv_data %>%
  group_by(Agency.Name) %>%
  summarise(predicted_mean = mean(predicted, na.rm = TRUE))

# combine
plot_df <- left_join(actual_means, predicted_means, by = "Agency.Name")

# export scatter plot as pdf for latex
pdf("[plot] linreg_department_means_1.pdf", width = 5, height = 4)

#plotting the means
plot(plot_df$actual_mean,
     plot_df$predicted_mean,
     xlab = "Actual Means",
     ylab = "Predicted Means",
     main = "Prorated Annual Means by Department",
     pch = 19,
     col = "blue")

# add 45-degree line
abline(0, 1, lty = 2, col = "red")

#close the export
dev.off()

# find the top 15 earning departments

plot_df %>%
  mutate(residual = actual_mean - predicted_mean) %>%
  arrange(desc(abs(residual))) %>%
  slice(1:15) %>%
  select(Agency.Name, actual_mean, predicted_mean, residual)

# store MAE, RMSE, Adjusted R^2
pred <- predict(model)
res  <- residuals(model)

MAE <- mean(abs(res))
RMSE <- sqrt(mean(res^2)) # root mean squared error
adj_r2 <- summary(model)$adj.r.squared # adjusted R^2

# print them to paste into the report

print("MAE")
print(MAE)
print("RMSE")
print(RMSE)
print("adjusted R^2")
print(adj_r2)

library(xtable)

xtable(summary(model))

```
